Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Sakae Holdings Ltd

Facts.Text: Sakae Holdings Ltd was a shareholder of Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, holding a minority shareholding relative to Gryphon Real Estate Investment Corporation Pte Ltd.

Sakae Holdings Ltd and ERC Holdings Pte Ltd invested in Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd as a joint venture vehicle to acquire most of the units in a commercial building known as Bugis Cube.

Sakae Holdings Ltd and ERC Holdings Pte Ltd entered into a joint venture arrangement recorded in a Joint Venture Agreement which included clauses 11.2 and 12.2 governing directors' contractual duties in relation to the joint venture company.
Douglas Foo Peow Yong served as a director and chairman of Sakae Holdings Ltd.

An option described as the ERC Option was treated as exercisable by ERC Holdings Pte Ltd to subscribe for additional shares in Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, which would require Sakae Holdings Ltd to subscribe for additional shares to maintain its percentage shareholding.

Sakae Holdings Ltd paid subscription money of $2,641,975 to Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd to subscribe for 2,641,975 ordinary shares in that company in order to maintain its percentage shareholding when ERC Holdings Pte Ltd exercised the ERC Option.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Sakae Holdings Ltd suffered financial and shareholder-value impact from transactions and share issuances involving Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd and ERC Holdings Pte Ltd, including the exercise of the ERC Option and related capital subscriptions.
Party_Details.Rule: A shareholder may seek relief where the conduct of a company's affairs or the exercise of directors' powers results in prejudice to the shareholder's interests, and directors are generally act with reasonable diligence, maintain adequate knowledge of the company's business, and ensure that share issuances and related-party transactions are implemented in accordance with agreed contractual frameworks and corporate law requirements.
Party_Details.Application: Sakae Holdings Ltd, as a minority shareholder in Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd under a joint venture structure with ERC Holdings Pte Ltd, participated in capital-raising events including the exercise of the ERC Option by ERC Holdings Pte Ltd. To maintain its agreed percentage shareholding, Sakae Holdings Ltd subscribed for additional shares and paid $2,641,975 to the joint venture company, thereby increasing its capital exposure to the joint venture vehicle.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court described the consolidated actions as arising from a dispute regarding the joint venture company Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, in which Sakae and ERC Holdings invested to acquire Bugis Cube. In Suit 122, Sakae pursued a claim against Ong Siew Kwee (Andy Ong) for breach of fiduciary duty as a director of Sakae in relation to share options granted by the joint venture company to another company in which he had an interest. In Suit 1098, Sakae pursued claims against, among others, Ong Siew Kwee, Ho Yew Kong, and Ong Han Boon for conducting the joint venture company's affairs in a manner amounting to oppression and unfair prejudice to Sakae as a shareholder. The defendants denied liability and commenced third party proceedings against Douglas Foo Peow Yong, alleging that he breached fiduciary duties owed to the joint venture company and that such breaches contributed to the transactions complained of by Sakae. The court had previously, in an earlier judgment, found Ong Siew Kwee, Ho Yew Kong, and Ong Han Boon liable to Sakae and the joint venture company for various acts of oppression and ordered them to pay specified sums to the joint venture company and to Sakae. In the present judgment, the court considered whether those defendants, as third party plaintiffs, could obtain contribution from Douglas Foo under sections 15 and 16 of the Civil Law Act. The court applied the three-step test from Tan Juay Pah v Kimly Construction Pte Ltd and Royal Brompton Hospital NHS Trust v Hammond to determine whether Douglas Foo was liable in respect of the same damage as the third party plaintiffs. The court analysed the nature of Sakae's damage in the oppression suit, characterising it as the loss of the basis on which Sakae invested in the joint venture company and the subversion of its right to fair management of the company's affairs, rather than simply the quantum of funds paid out of the company. The court held that any breach of duty by Douglas Foo as a director of the joint venture company, based on inaction or reliance on the management agreement and on Ong Siew Kwee, did not render him liable to Sakae under section 216 of the Companies Act because oppression under that provision requires active conduct of the company's affairs or active exercise of powers in an oppressive or prejudicial manner. The court found no evidence that Douglas Foo had knowledge of the oppressive activities and rejected the argument that his failure to be more active could ground liability to Sakae for the same damage as that caused by the third party plaintiffs. The court further reasoned that even if the joint venture company could have a claim against Douglas Foo for breach of duty, any liability he might have to the company would not equate to liability to Sakae for the same damage as that caused by the third party plaintiffs. The court then examined specific transactions, including excessive management fees paid to Gryphon Capital Management Pte Ltd, the First Loan and related documentation, the Lease Agreement and its termination, the Consultancy Agreement, and the May PMA. In each instance, the court characterised the harm as arising from deliberate conduct and the use of sham or ex post facto documentation by the third party plaintiffs, and concluded that it would be inappropriate and inequitable to require Douglas Foo to contribute to liabilities arising from such conduct. The court also relied on section 16(2) of the Civil Law Act to exempt Douglas Foo from contribution even if some responsibility could be attributed to him. Regarding Suit 122 and the ERC Option, the court reiterated that Ong Siew Kwee's liability to Sakae arose from his failure, as Sakae's director, to provide relevant information and from misleading Sakae and Douglas Foo about the existence and validity of the option. The court noted that if a valid option had existed, Douglas Foo's signing of documents to give effect to it would have been in the joint venture company's interests, and that the company was not harmed by the share issuance because it received additional capital from both ERC Holdings and Sakae. The court found that Sakae's loss consisted of the additional capital it invested, and that Ong Siew Kwee had not established any breach of fiduciary duty by Douglas Foo to Sakae that would justify contribution. The court indicated that, in any event, it would have exercised its discretion under section 16(2) of the Civil Law Act to exempt Douglas Foo from contribution, given that Ong Siew Kwee was the intended beneficiary of the increased shareholding and had misled Douglas Foo. On the procedural defence, the court considered arguments that Ong Siew Kwee and Ong Han Boon had not opened their case or adduced evidence in the third party proceedings and that their claims should therefore be dismissed. While expressing sympathy for Douglas Foo's position, the court held that third party proceedings are closely connected to the main action and that parties may rely on evidence adduced in the main action, including evidence from the third party himself, to support or defend third party claims. The court therefore declined to dismiss the claims solely on procedural grounds and proceeded to determine them on their merits. Ultimately, the court dismissed all third party claims by Ong Siew Kwee, Ong Han Boon, and Ho Yew Kong against Douglas Foo. On costs, the court considered whether costs should be awarded on the indemnity basis under Order 59 rule 5 of the Rules of Court, taking into account the conduct of the parties and the principles discussed in Tan Chin Yew Joseph v Saxo Capital Markets Pte Ltd, Macmillan Inc v Bishopsgate Investment Trust plc, and Three Rivers District Council v The Governor and Company of the Bank of England (No 6). The court reviewed the conduct of the third party plaintiffs, including the stated purpose at a judicial pre-trial conference of commencing third party proceedings to compel Douglas Foo to testify, their decision not to open their case or adduce evidence while still pursuing the claims, the nature and credibility of the evidence advanced by Ho Yew Kong, and the fact that they did not seek contribution from each other despite their own reliance on one another in the impugned transactions. While acknowledging that bringing a weak case or choosing particular defendants is not in itself unreasonable, the court found that, on the facts established in the earlier judgment, the impugned transactions had been orchestrated by the third party plaintiffs to benefit Ong Siew Kwee's other interests without regard to Sakae and the joint venture company, and that they had taken advantage of the trust placed in them by Douglas Foo. The court concluded that bringing third party claims against Douglas Foo in these circumstances amounted to suing in bad faith and justified an award of indemnity costs. The court therefore ordered the third party plaintiffs to bear Douglas Foo's costs of defending the third party claims on the indemnity basis.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Gryphon Real Estate Investment Corporation Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Gryphon Real Estate Investment Corporation Pte Ltd was a shareholder of Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd alongside Sakae Holdings Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the manner in which Gryphon Real Estate Investment Corporation Pte Ltd participated in the affairs of Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd affected the financial and governance position of Sakae Holdings Ltd as a minority shareholder.
Party_Details.Rule: Where multiple shareholders participate in a joint venture company, the conduct of each shareholder in relation to the company's affairs may be assessed against standards of fair treatment and compliance with agreed joint venture arrangements.
Party_Details.Application: Gryphon Real Estate Investment Corporation Pte Ltd held shares in Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd and, together with Sakae Holdings Ltd, formed the shareholder base within which control and management of the joint venture company were exercised.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment refers to Gryphon Real Estate Investment Corporation Pte Ltd as the other shareholder in Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd whose interests were contrasted with those of Sakae Holdings Ltd in the oppression analysis. The court treated the damage to Sakae as arising from the subversion of its right to have the company's affairs managed as fairly in its interests as in the interests of Gryphon Real Estate Investment Corporation Pte Ltd. No specific liability findings or contribution issues were determined in this judgment in relation to Gryphon Real Estate Investment Corporation Pte Ltd.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: ERC Holdings Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: ERC Holdings Pte Ltd, a company belonging to Ong Siew Kwee, invested in Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd together with Sakae Holdings Ltd to acquire most of the units in Bugis Cube.

ERC Holdings Pte Ltd was a party to a Joint Venture Agreement with Sakae Holdings Ltd governing their investment in Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, including provisions on share options and shareholding percentages.
ERC Holdings Pte Ltd was treated as holding an option, referred to as the ERC Option, to subscribe for additional shares in Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd.
ERC Holdings Pte Ltd subscribed for 8,058,025 ordinary shares in Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd pursuant to the ERC Option, providing subscription money to the company.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether ERC Holdings Pte Ltd's exercise of the ERC Option and participation in related share issuances altered the agreed shareholding balance and financial contributions under the joint venture with Sakae Holdings Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: In a joint venture governed by a contractual agreement, the exercise of share options and issuance of new shares are generally comply with the agreed terms and to preserve or adjust shareholding proportions in accordance with the contractual framework.
Party_Details.Application: ERC Holdings Pte Ltd, controlled by Ong Siew Kwee, exercised the ERC Option to subscribe for additional shares in Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, leading to a corresponding subscription by Sakae Holdings Ltd to maintain its percentage shareholding and increasing both parties' capital contributions to the joint venture company.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court discussed the ERC Option in the context of Suit 122, noting that ERC Holdings Pte Ltd was treated as having an option to subscribe for additional shares in Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd and that its exercise of this option led to Sakae Holdings Ltd subscribing for additional shares to maintain its percentage shareholding. The court's liability analysis in Suit 122 focused on the conduct of Ong Siew Kwee as a director of Sakae, including his failure to inform Sakae about the option and his role in leading Sakae and Douglas Foo to believe that a valid option existed. The judgment did not impose contribution liability on ERC Holdings Pte Ltd in the third party proceedings and did not address ERC Holdings Pte Ltd's direct liability in this segment.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Ong Siew Kwee (Andy Ong)

Facts.Text: Ong Siew Kwee, also known as Andy Ong, was the third defendant in Suit 1098 of 2013 and the defendant in Suit 122 of 2013.
Ong Siew Kwee was a director of Sakae Holdings Ltd during the period the joint venture with ERC Holdings Pte Ltd and Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd.

Ong Siew Kwee was a director of Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd during the period when the joint venture company acquired units in Bugis Cube and entered into the impugned transactions.
ERC Holdings Pte Ltd, which invested in Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd alongside Sakae Holdings Ltd, was a company belonging to Ong Siew Kwee.

Ong Siew Kwee participated in the negotiation and implementation of the Joint Venture Agreement between Sakae Holdings Ltd and ERC Holdings Pte Ltd relating to Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the pre-dispute conduct of Ong Siew Kwee in his roles with Sakae Holdings Ltd, ERC Holdings Pte Ltd, and Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, including his involvement in joint venture arrangements and share option structures, created exposure to later claims concerning capital contributions and management of the joint venture company.
Party_Details.Rule: Individuals who hold directorships or controlling positions in multiple entities within a joint venture structure are generally manage overlapping roles in a way that aligns with agreed contractual terms and corporate governance standards, particularly where share issuances and related-party transactions may affect shareholder positions.
Party_Details.Application: As a director of both Sakae Holdings Ltd and Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd and as the controlling person of ERC Holdings Pte Ltd, Ong Siew Kwee was centrally involved in establishing and operating the joint venture structure, including the Joint Venture Agreement and the ERC Option mechanism, which later formed the basis of disputes over share subscriptions and the management of the joint venture company's affairs.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: In the earlier main judgment referenced in this decision, the court held that Ong Siew Kwee was liable to Sakae Holdings Ltd and Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd for various acts of oppression and ordered him to pay specified sums to the joint venture company and to Sakae. The present judgment focuses on his attempts, together with other defendants, to obtain contribution from Douglas Foo under sections 15 and 16 of the Civil Law Act. The court recounted that third party notices were filed by Ong Siew Kwee in both Suit 1098 and Suit 122 seeking indemnity or contribution from Douglas Foo in respect of any liability to Sakae. The court noted that in closing submissions the third party plaintiffs abandoned their indemnity claims and pursued only contribution. The court analysed the statutory framework for contribution and emphasised the requirement that both the claimant and the person from whom contribution is sought be liable in respect of the same damage. Applying the three-step test from Tan Juay Pah and Royal Brompton, the court concluded that the damage suffered by Sakae in the oppression suit was not simply the sums paid out of the joint venture company but the loss of its expectation of fair management and the diminution in value of its shareholding. The court held that any inaction by Douglas Foo as a director of the joint venture company did not render him liable to Sakae under section 216 of the Companies Act and therefore did not satisfy the same damage requirement. The court further held that the impugned transactions, including excessive management fees, the First Loan, the Lease Agreement, the Consultancy Agreement, and the May PMA, involved deliberate conduct and the use of sham or ex post facto documentation by Ong Siew Kwee and his associates. The court reasoned that allowing contribution from Douglas Foo in respect of liabilities arising from such conduct would effectively reduce the responsibility of those who orchestrated the transactions. In relation to Suit 122 and the ERC Option, the court reiterated that Ong Siew Kwee's liability to Sakae arose from his conduct as Sakae's director, including his failure to inform Sakae about the option and his role in leading Sakae and Douglas Foo to believe that a valid option existed. The court found that Sakae's loss consisted of the additional capital it invested and that Ong Siew Kwee had not established any breach of fiduciary duty by Douglas Foo to Sakae that would justify contribution. The court indicated that, even if some basis for contribution existed, it would exercise its discretion under section 16(2) of the Civil Law Act to exempt Douglas Foo, given that Ong Siew Kwee was the intended beneficiary of the increased shareholding and had misled Douglas Foo. The court also addressed a procedural defence based on Ong Siew Kwee and Ong Han Boon's decision not to open their case or adduce evidence in the third party proceedings, ultimately deciding to consider the claims on their merits while expressing concern about the fairness of relying solely on evidence from the main action. On costs, the court found that the third party claims were brought and pursued in circumstances amounting to bad faith, given the findings in the main judgment about the orchestration of the impugned transactions and the exploitation of Douglas Foo's trust. The court therefore ordered that the third party plaintiffs, including Ong Siew Kwee, pay Douglas Foo's costs of the third party proceedings on an indemnity basis.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Ho Yew Kong

Facts.Text: Ho Yew Kong was the fourth defendant in Suit 1098 of 2013.
Ho Yew Kong was a director of Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd during the period when the company entered into transactions later challenged by Sakae Holdings Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the pre-dispute conduct of Ho Yew Kong as a director of Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, in the context of the joint venture between Sakae Holdings Ltd and ERC Holdings Pte Ltd, contributed to later disputes over transactions and management of the joint venture company.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors of a joint venture company are generally act with reasonable diligence and to consider the interests of the company as a whole when participating in transactions and approving documentation.
Party_Details.Application: As a director of Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, Ho Yew Kong participated in the management of the joint venture company alongside other directors associated with Sakae Holdings Ltd and ERC Holdings Pte Ltd, in a context where multiple related-party transactions and agreements were later scrutinised.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: In the earlier main judgment, the court held that Ho Yew Kong was jointly and severally liable with other defendants for certain sums to be paid to Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd arising from transactions such as the Lease Agreement, the Consultancy Agreement, and the May PMA. In the present judgment, the court considered his third party claim against Douglas Foo for contribution. The court summarised his distinct submissions, including arguments that Douglas Foo had participated in creating a structure in which he abdicated his role to Ong Siew Kwee and failed to work with Ho Yew Kong, and that Ho Yew Kong was entitled to assume that internal management matters within Sakae Holdings Ltd had been complied with in relation to related-party transactions. The court characterised these submissions as a variant of the Established Norm defence previously rejected in the main judgment. It held that Ho Yew Kong had a duty to act independently in the interests of the joint venture company and that, knowing that Douglas Foo had largely left management to Ong Siew Kwee, he should have alerted Douglas Foo if he was aware of conduct adverse to the company's interests. Instead, the court found that he cooperated with Ong Siew Kwee and signed documents, including those described as sham agreements, without applying an independent mind. The court concluded that he was not in a better position than Ong Siew Kwee to claim contribution from Douglas Foo and dismissed his third party claim. On costs, the court included Ho Yew Kong among the third party plaintiffs ordered to pay Douglas Foo's costs on an indemnity basis, citing the overall bad faith context of the impugned transactions and the third party proceedings.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Ong Han Boon

Facts.Text: Ong Han Boon was the fifth defendant in Suit 1098 of 2013.
Ong Han Boon was a director of Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd during the period when the company entered into transactions later challenged by Sakae Holdings Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the pre-dispute conduct of Ong Han Boon as a director of Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, in conjunction with other directors, contributed to later disputes over management fees, loans, and other transactions involving the joint venture company.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors who participate in the management of a company are generally review and understand significant transactions and fee arrangements and to ensure that company funds are applied consistently with the company's interests and agreed frameworks.
Party_Details.Application: As a director of Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, Ong Han Boon was involved in the governance of the joint venture company alongside other directors associated with Sakae Holdings Ltd and ERC Holdings Pte Ltd, in a setting where management fee arrangements and loan structures were later examined in litigation.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: In the earlier main judgment, the court held that Ong Han Boon was jointly and severally liable with Ong Siew Kwee for certain sums to be paid to Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, including amounts related to excessive management fees and the First Loan. In the present judgment, the court considered his third party claim against Douglas Foo for contribution. The court noted that Ong Han Boon, like Ong Siew Kwee, did not open his case or adduce evidence in the third party proceedings and instead sought to rely on evidence from the main action, including evidence given by Douglas Foo. The court expressed concern about the fairness of this approach but ultimately decided that it could not dismiss the claims solely on that basis. On the merits, the court rejected the argument that any failure by Douglas Foo to be more active as a director of the joint venture company could ground contribution liability in respect of deliberate conduct by Ong Han Boon and others, such as the management fee arrangements and the First Loan. The court emphasised that the harm arose from deliberate actions and documentation and that allowing contribution would improperly reduce the responsibility of those who implemented the transactions. The court therefore dismissed Ong Han Boon's third party claim and included him among the parties ordered to pay Douglas Foo's costs on an indemnity basis.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd was the joint venture company established for the acquisition of most of the units in the commercial building known as Bugis Cube.
Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd had Sakae Holdings Ltd and ERC Holdings Pte Ltd as its principal investing shareholders under a joint venture arrangement.

Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd was a Joint Venture Agreement between its shareholders Sakae Holdings Ltd and ERC Holdings Pte Ltd, which included provisions on share options and directors' contractual duties.
Douglas Foo Peow Yong served as a director of Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd at all material times the joint venture and the transactions later in dispute.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the structure and governance of Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, as a joint venture vehicle for acquiring Bugis Cube, created conditions that later gave rise to disputes between its shareholders over transactions and capital contributions.
Party_Details.Rule: Joint venture companies formed to hold and manage specific assets are typically governed by shareholder agreements and company law, which together regulate share issuances, director conduct, and the handling of company funds.
Party_Details.Application: Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd functioned as the joint venture vehicle through which Sakae Holdings Ltd and ERC Holdings Pte Ltd acquired units in Bugis Cube, with directors drawn from both shareholder groups and the Joint Venture Agreement and statutory duties, forming the corporate context for the later disputes.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment treats Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd as the central joint venture company whose affairs were the subject of the oppression claims in Suit 1098. The court ordered various defendants to pay sums to this company in the earlier main judgment as part of the relief granted in connection with the winding up of the company. In the present judgment, the court emphasised that any potential breach of duty by Douglas Foo as a director would give rise, if at all, to a claim by the company rather than to liability to Sakae Holdings Ltd for the same damage as that caused by the third party plaintiffs. The court's analysis of contribution under the Civil Law Act repeatedly distinguished between damage to the company and damage to Sakae as a shareholder.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Gryphon Capital Management Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Gryphon Capital Management Pte Ltd provided management services to Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd and received management, ancillary, and administrative fees recorded in the accounts of both companies.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the management fee arrangements between Gryphon Capital Management Pte Ltd and Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, as reflected in their accounts, contributed to later disputes over the quantum and basis of fees paid.
Party_Details.Rule: Management companies engaged by a principal company are generally charge fees in accordance with agreed terms, and such fees are typically recorded in the management accounts of both entities.
Party_Details.Application: Gryphon Capital Management Pte Ltd received fees from Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd that were recorded in both entities' management accounts, forming part of the factual matrix later examined in litigation concerning the level and justification of those fees.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court referred to Gryphon Capital Management Pte Ltd in the context of excessive management fees paid by Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, for which certain defendants were held liable in the main judgment. In the present judgment, the court discussed these fees when analysing the third party plaintiffs' attempt to attribute responsibility to Douglas Foo on the basis that he should have been aware of the fee levels from the management accounts. The court rejected the argument that any failure by Douglas Foo to detect or prevent the fee payments could ground contribution liability in respect of deliberate conduct by the third party plaintiffs.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Douglas Foo Peow Yong

Facts.Text: Douglas Foo Peow Yong served as a director and chairman of Sakae Holdings Ltd during the period the joint venture with ERC Holdings Pte Ltd and Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd.

Douglas Foo Peow Yong was a director of Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd at all material times during which the company acquired units in Bugis Cube and entered into the transactions later in dispute.

Douglas Foo Peow Yong signed directors' resolutions of Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd approving the issue and allotment of 8,058,025 ordinary shares to ERC Holdings Pte Ltd and 2,641,975 ordinary shares to Sakae Holdings Ltd in connection with the exercise of the ERC Option.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on his pre-dispute conduct as a director of Sakae Holdings Ltd and Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, Douglas Foo Peow Yong could be exposed to contribution claims for the same damage as that for which other directors were later held responsible.
Party_Details.Rule: Under contribution principles, a person may be contribute to another's liability only where both are in respect of the same damage, and courts may exempt a person from contribution where it is just and equitable to do so.
Party_Details.Application: Douglas Foo Peow Yong held directorships in both Sakae Holdings Ltd and Griffin Real Estate Investment Holdings Pte Ltd and signed corporate resolutions implementing share issuances associated with the ERC Option, but his role was later assessed in contribution proceedings to determine whether his conduct created overlapping liability with that of other directors.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court considered third party claims brought by Ong Siew Kwee, Ong Han Boon, and Ho Yew Kong against Douglas Foo under sections 15 and 16 of the Civil Law Act, seeking contribution in respect of liabilities imposed on them in the main judgment. The court set out the statutory framework, emphasising the requirement that both the claimant and the person from whom contribution is sought be liable in respect of the same damage and noting the court's power under section 16(2) to exempt a person from contribution. Applying the three-step test from Tan Juay Pah and Royal Brompton, the court analysed the nature of the damage suffered by Sakae Holdings Ltd in the oppression suit and concluded that it consisted of the loss of the basis on which Sakae invested in the joint venture company and the subversion of its right to fair management, rather than simply the sums paid out of the company. The court held that any inaction by Douglas Foo as a director of the joint venture company, such as relying on the management agreement and leaving day-to-day management to Ong Siew Kwee, did not amount to conduct that could render him liable to Sakae under section 216 of the Companies Act, which requires active conduct of the company's affairs or active exercise of powers in an oppressive or prejudicial manner. The court found no evidence that Douglas Foo had knowledge of the oppressive activities and rejected the argument that his failure to be more active could ground liability to Sakae for the same damage as that caused by the third party plaintiffs. The court further reasoned that any potential breach of duty by Douglas Foo would give rise, if at all, to a claim by the joint venture company rather than to liability to Sakae for the same damage. The court then examined specific transactions, including excessive management fees, the First Loan, the Lease Agreement, the Consultancy Agreement, and the May PMA, and concluded that the harm arose from deliberate conduct and the use of sham or ex post facto documentation by the third party plaintiffs. It held that allowing contribution from Douglas Foo in respect of liabilities arising from such conduct would be inappropriate and inequitable and that, in any event, he should be exempted from contribution under section 16(2) of the Civil Law Act. In relation to Suit 122 and the ERC Option, the court noted that Douglas Foo had signed directors' resolutions implementing the share issuances but found that the joint venture company was not harmed by the share issuance because it received additional capital from both ERC Holdings Pte Ltd and Sakae Holdings Ltd. The court held that Sakae's loss consisted of the additional capital it invested and that no breach of fiduciary duty by Douglas Foo to Sakae had been established that would justify contribution. The court indicated that, even if some basis for contribution existed, it would exercise its discretion to exempt him, given that he had been misled by Ong Siew Kwee and that Ong Siew Kwee was the intended beneficiary of the increased shareholding. On a procedural defence, the court considered arguments that the third party plaintiffs had not opened their case or adduced evidence in the third party proceedings and that their claims should therefore be dismissed. While expressing sympathy for Douglas Foo's position, the court held that third party proceedings are closely connected to the main action and that parties may rely on evidence from the main action, including evidence from the third party, to support or defend third party claims. The court therefore declined to dismiss the claims solely on procedural grounds but ultimately dismissed them on their merits. On costs, the court found that the third party claims were brought and pursued in circumstances amounting to bad faith, given the findings in the main judgment about the orchestration of the impugned transactions and the exploitation of Douglas Foo's trust. The court ordered that the third party plaintiffs pay Douglas Foo's costs of the third party proceedings on an indemnity basis.
